content: 5 min read 1 hour ago -- A structured tour of the things that actually make sites fast — the critical rendering path, bundle size, lazy loading, and the Core Web Vitals Google scores you on — minus the hand-waving. Press enter or click to view image in full size Most “make your site faster” advice is a pile of disconnected tips: lazy-load images, minify JS, add a CDN. Useful, but you end up cargo-culting without a mental model. This is the map I wish someone had handed me early — a tour from the moment bytes leave the server to the moment the user can actually use your page, with the levers that matter at each stage. Treat this as a hub. Each section is a thing you can go deep on, and Frontend Master’s deep dives are linked as we go. Stage 1 — The Critical Rendering Path Everything starts here. When the browser receives your HTML, it builds the DOM. When it hits a , it builds the CSSOM. Then it combines them into a render tree, lays it out, and paints. Pixels. The two things that block this path: • CSS is render-blocking. The browser won’t paint until it has the CSSOM, because it refuses to show unstyled content. A giant styles.css in the delays first paint. • Synchronous JS is parser-blocking. A plain If you understand the critical rendering path, half of “performance best practices” stops being magic and becomes obvious. Stage 2 — Bundle size, the silent tax Every kilobyte of JavaScript is downloaded, parsed, compiled, and executed — and parse/compile happen on the main thread. A 2MB bundle isn’t just a slow download; it’s hundreds of milliseconds of CPU before your app does anything, especially on mid-range phones. Three habits keep bundles honest: • Code-split by route. The login page shouldn’t ship the dashboard’s charting library. • Audit your dependencies. That one date helper might be pulling in a 70KB locale bundle. Run a bundle analyzer and look at the treemap. • Compress the bytes you do ship. Text compression alone often cuts JS payloads by 70–80%. That last one is worth its own deep dive — Brotli vs Gzip, compression levels, and pre-compressing at build time vs compressing on the fly. Frontend Master breaks it all down in Brotli and Gzip compression for JS bundles; it’s the single highest-leverage server-side change most teams haven’t made. Stage 3 — Load less, later (lazy loading) You don’t need everything at first paint. The discipline is shipping the critical path now and deferring the rest. // Route-level code splitting (the dynamic import is the key) const Settings = lazy(() => import('./Settings'));A subtle gotcha: lazy-loading the wrong things hurts. If you lazy-load your hero image or your largest above-the-fold element, you delay the very thing the user is waiting for. Lazy load what’s off-screen; prioritize what’s on-screen with fetchpriority="high". Same tool, opposite outcomes depending on judgment. Stage 4 — Core Web Vitals: the scoreboard You can’t manage what you don’t measure, and Google measures three things on real users: • LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) — how fast the biggest above-the-fold element renders. Target: under 2.5s. Usually your hero image or headline. • CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift) — how much the page jumps around as it loads. Target: under 0.1. Caused by images without dimensions, injected banners, and late-loading fonts. • INP (Interaction to Next Paint) — how quickly the page responds to clicks and taps. Target: under 200ms. This is where heavy main-thread work (think un-chunked JSON parsing or a slow click handler) shows up. If you’re new to these, start with the Core Web Vitals intro — it explains each metric and what moves it before you try to optimize anything. Stage 5 — Measure, don’t guess This is the meta-skill that ties the whole crash course together, and it’s the one most people skip. There are two kinds of data, and you need both: • Lab data — Lighthouse, WebPageTest, the DevTools Performance panel. Reproducible, controlled, great for debugging why. But it’s one device, one network, one moment. • Field data — real users, real devices, real networks. Chrome’s CrUX report and the web-vitals library tell you what's actually happening in production. import { onLCP, onCLS, onINP } from 'web-vitals';onLCP(console.log); onCLS(console.log); onINP(console.log); // Ship these to your analytics and watch the 75th percentile, not the average.The number that matters is usually the 75th percentile of real users — because performance problems live in the tail, on the slow phone with three apps open, not on your M-series laptop on office wifi. If you optimize against your own machine, you’ll “fix” things your users never struggled with and miss the ones they do. A working order of operations When a page feels slow, I work it in this sequence: • Measure in the field — which metric is actually bad (LCP, CLS, INP)? • Reproduce in the lab — record a Performance trace, find the long task or the late paint. • Attack the biggest block — render-blocking CSS/JS, oversized bundle, an unoptimized LCP image, a layout shift. • Re-measure. Confirm the field metric moved. If it didn’t, you fixed the wrong thing. That loop — measure, reproduce, fix, re-measure — is the entire game. Tips without it are guesses. Where to go from here This crash course is the trunk; the specific topics are branches. Compression, Core Web Vitals, font loading, and main-thread work each deserve their own session. Start with whichever metric your field data says is worst. Watch the full crash course The complete video walks through every stage above with live demos and DevTools traces: The Web Performance Crash Course. Frontend Master posts new frontend deep-dives weekly on @rahuulmiishra — subscribe if this was useful. And if you want a guided performance audit of your own app or interview prep on this exact topic, Frontend Master runs 1:1 mock interviews — book one at allahabadi.dev/frontend-mock-interview.
